topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed on my monitor, a stark contrast to the knot tightening in my stomach. I scrolled through the quarterly reports, the numbers blurring until I landed on the salary comparison. Liam’s figure, tucked neatly in the corner, was significantly higher than mine. A slow, cold dread washed over me. I kept my gaze fixed on the data, my fingers hovering over the keyboard as if afraid to touch anything else. Every time I glanced toward his desk, a sharp, unpleasant pressure built behind my ribs. I started re-reading emails, dissecting every interaction, searching for some oversight, some mistake that would explain this imbalance. When he finally approached me with a casual query about a project, my voice came out tight and strained. "Oh, yeah, I can handle that," I managed, forcing a smile that felt brittle on my face. Internally, I felt a heavy weight settling onto my shoulders, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

I spent the afternoon meticulously reviewing my expense reports, a distraction I desperately needed. The figures kept pulling my attention back to the discrepancy. It wasn't a massive gap, just enough to feel like a deliberate slight. I kept catching myself staring at the window, watching the city bustle, feeling a strange hollowness. When Sarah mentioned a recent promotion she’d received, the air in the office seemed to thicken. I offered a weak nod, my hands gripping my pen so tightly my knuckles turned white. Later, when I was alone in the breakroom, I traced patterns on the condensation of my water glass. A wave of self-reproach washed over me—a deep, uncomfortable acknowledgment of my own perceived shortcomings. I wanted to apologize, to smooth things over, but the words felt stuck, trapped behind a sudden dryness in my throat.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the office seemed too bright, exposing every flaw in my composure. I kept glancing toward the corner office where Mark worked, a silent assessment of his success weighing heavily on me. I tried to focus on the task at hand—filing documents—but my concentration fractured repeatedly. Every time a notification popped up showing a recent bonus announcement, a sharp, acidic taste rose in my mouth. I found myself avoiding eye contact in meetings, my posture stiff and withdrawn. When my supervisor praised a recent team achievement, I managed a polite, almost mechanical response. Inside, a churning sensation took hold; a profound sense of being overshadowed settled deep within my core. I spent the rest of the day replaying casual conversations, trying to find some evidence that I had somehow missed a crucial piece of information, anything to justify the feeling of inadequacy churning relentlessly in my chest.
